did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used computer vision and machine learning techniques for melanoma detection from dermoscopic images. Specifically, it employed feature extraction (ABCD features and others) followed by classification using multiple machine learning models including Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbour, Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes, Artificial Neural Network (ANN), AdaBoost, and Gradient Boosting. A voting-based ensemble method was also utilized to combine classifier outputs.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a computer vision system for melanoma detection. It details a specific methodology including image pre-processing, feature extraction (ABCD and other dermoscopic features), and classification using multiple machine learning models (SVM, Decision Tree, KNN, etc.) and a voting-based ensemble method. The paper reports original results from evaluating these classifiers on a dataset using 10-fold cross-validation, with specific accuracy, precision, recall, and F-Score metrics provided in Table 2. Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to describe and validate a new diagnostic approach, not to summarize prior literature.

title: Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection from Dermoscopic Images using Computer Vision
authors: Jayatilake S. M. D. A. C.
journal: 2nd International Conference on Image Processing and Robotics (ICIPRob)
brief_summary: This study developed a computer vision system for early melanoma detection from dermoscopic images by extracting salient dermoscopic features, including ABCD criteria and other visible lesion characteristics. The methodology involved pre-processing steps such as hair and ink removal, followed by feature extraction and classification using multiple machine learning models like SVM, Decision Tree, KNN, Logistic Regression, ANN, AdaBoost, and Gradient Boosting. A voting-based ensemble method was employed to combine classifier outputs, excluding Naive Bayes due to poor performance. The system was evaluated on a balanced subset of 2,200 images from the HAM10000 dataset using 10-fold cross-validation. The voting-based ensemble method achieved the highest performance with 88% accuracy, 87% precision, 93% recall, and an 89% F-Score. The authors conclude that this approach offers a reliable, efficient, and cost-effective alternative to manual diagnosis for clinical practice.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The study focuses on diagnosing Melanoma using dermoscopic images and extracted features (ABCD criteria and others).
Used as the negative class in a balanced dataset subset from HAM10000. The paper explicitly lists these specific lesion types as comprising the non-melanoma group used in the experiments.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task to diagnose whether a patient has Melanoma or not (Non-melanoma) based on extracted dermoscopic features. The study evaluates multiple classifiers (SVM, Decision Tree, KNN, Logistic Regression, ANN, AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting) and a voting-based ensemble method on a balanced dataset of Melanoma and Non-melanoma lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. The output is a diagnostic label with a confidence level.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used due to its diverse range of images captured from various locations under different lighting and camera conditions.
classes_used: Non-melanoma (Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevi, Dermatofibroma)

use.training: Used to create a balanced dataset subset of 2,200 images for training the classification models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It compared classifier performance using descriptive metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F-Score) and identified the best-performing model based on these values without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000 (Balanced Subset of 2,200 Image.notes: The voting-based ensemble method achieved the highest overall performance. Naive Bayes was excluded from the ensemble due to poor evaluation results. SVM was identified as the second-best suited classifier. The study notes that an average accuracy of 88% and an average F-measure of 89% were achieved across the evaluated models.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple traditional machine learning classifiers and a voting-based ensemble method
architecture_used.input_size: null (Input consists of extracted dermoscopic features, not raw image pixels)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: 12 dermoscopic features extracted using Python OpenCV: Asymmetry, Border Irregularity, Colour Variation, Diameter, Atypical Pigment Network, Irregular Streaks, Regression Structure, Blue-White Veil, Milky-Red Areas, Globules, Blotches, and Rosettes.
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Voting-based ensemble method combining outputs of all classifiers except Naive Bayes

hyperparameters.AdaBoost: Number of models to iteratively train set to 28

training_details.dataset: Balanced subset of 2,200 images from HAM10000 (1,100 Melanoma, 1,100 Non-melanoma)

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal: Image converted to grayscale; morphological transformation and thresholding applied to detect hair; hair pixels removed and filled with mix of colors from neighboring pixels.

Ink patches removal: Image converted to Hue Saturation Value (HSV) color space; upper and lower threshold values set to detect ink marks; confirmed via second color range check; ink pixels removed and filled with mix of neighboring pixel colors.

Lens frame removal: Image converted to HSV color space to detect black color; lower and upper threshold values set; margins calculated on three points per side (first quarter, middle, last quarter); image cropped to remove dermatoscope frame.

Skin lesion extraction (Segmentation): Image converted to grayscale; dynamic threshold values and geometrical factors used to capture contours; best suited contour selected based on location and area; masking applied to isolate skin lesion.
Color thresholding for feature analysis: Predefined upper and lower threshold values for white, red, and blue colors used to detect color presence on the lesion.
conclusions: The proposed computer vision system, which extracts dermoscopic features (including ABCD criteria and others) and uses a voting-based ensemble method, achieved an accuracy of 88%, precision of 87%, recall of 93%, and an F-Score of 89% on a balanced subset of the HAM10000 dataset.

The voting-based ensemble method was identified as the best-suited classifier for melanoma diagnosis based on the evaluated dermoscopic features, outperforming individual classifiers like SVM, Decision Tree, Logistic Regression, KNN, ANN, AdaBoost, and Gradient Boosting.

The Support Vector Machine (SVM) was identified as the second-best suited classifier for the purpose, while Logistic Regression (LR) and Artificial Neural Network (ANN) showed quite similar evaluation results to SVM.
The Naive Bayes classifier was determined to be the least suited classifier due to poor evaluation results (73% accuracy, 65% F-Score) and was excluded from the voting-based ensemble method.

The study concludes that the suggested approach offers a reliable, efficient, and cost-effective alternative to manual diagnosis for clinical practice, with considerably improved detection rate, precision, recall, and F-score values compared to previous research.
The authors acknowledge that using a custom image dataset captured with a high-quality camera could significantly improve the accuracy of diameter feature extraction and overall solution performance.
The authors suggest that monitoring a series of images of skin lesions over time could be considered to capture lesion evolution and obtain better diagnosis results.
important_remarks: The study uses a balanced subset of only 2,200 images from the HAM10000 dataset (1,100 Melanoma, 1,100 Non-melanoma), which limits the generalizability compared to using the full dataset.

The Non-melanoma class is a heterogeneous group comprising Actinic Keratoses, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevi, and Dermatofibroma, which may affect the specificity of the 'Non-melanoma' label.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using 10-fold cross-validation on the internal subset of the HAM10000 dataset.
The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) to compare the performance of the classifiers.

The feature extraction relies on predefined threshold values for color and geometric properties, which may not be robust across different imaging devices or lighting conditions without further calibration.
The authors acknowledge that using a custom dataset captured with a high-quality camera could significantly improve the accuracy of diameter feature extraction.

The Naive Bayes classifier was excluded from the voting-based ensemble method due to poor performance (73% accuracy, 65% F-Score), indicating potential instability in ensemble construction if weak learners are not carefully selected.

The study focuses on binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma) rather than multi-class classification, limiting its clinical utility for distinguishing between specific non-melanoma skin cancers.
